data.title: Playing The Odds On U.S. Stocks In August And September 2025
data.description:
There is a seasonality in asset class investment performance that is revealed in the history of calendar month returns. Based on history, investors should be “all in” US stocks this August but “mostly out” in September. Over the past 99 years, the best Asset Class to invest in during August has been the US Total Stock Market. It has achieved an average return of 1.20%, and the investment has been profitable 66% of the time. By contrast, in September the US Total Stock market ranks 11th and is profitable only 50% of the time.

In the following, I report on the best and worst months for US stocks alone, and then I extend my analysis to include most asset classes, moving beyond US stocks. August has historically been a pretty good month for US stocks, but it is followed by the worst month for US stocks -- September.

Playing the US Stock Market Game

Playing the stock market is a gamble and each month this game has different odds for success, as shown in the following:

The best odds are in December when you have a 77% chance of making money – i.e., having a positive return. The odds are in your favor (better than 50/50) in most of the other months, except September when there’s only a 50/50 chance of profiting in the stock market.

In Las Vegas, skilled gamblers base their bets on the probability of winning. Should you play the September 2025 game? Should you bet the same as you do in the high odds months, like December?

August has been a good month with a 64% chance of a positive return, a little above the average 63% probability.

That Time of Year Again

It’s August-September again so any number of articles will remind us that September has been the worst month for US stock markets, but no one will comment on August as being a pretty good month. Here’s a recap on the history of monthly returns on the S&P500 over the past 99.5 years (1194 months):

Shown graphically, September leaps off the page as being the only month with an average negative return, and the magnitude of average loss is serious at -0.8%. While August’s 1.2% average return is above the overall average 1.0% monthly return.

September has been the worst performing month because:

  • It has the lowest average return. The average loss in September has been -0,8%, due in part to the fact that the worst month ever happened in a September. A 29.7% loss in September 1931 is the worst monthly loss ever. All of the other 11 months have positive average returns.
  • 49 of the past 99 Septembers – 49.5% -- have suffered losses, contrasted to the other months that have had positive returns 64% of the time.

Momentum or Reversal

The US stock market has earned 7.86% so far this year, so on pace for a 14% annual 2025 return. Based on history, August should see a continuation of the bull run. But this September could disappoint if the current bull market reverses, or it will surprise to the upside if the bull runs. Which do you think will be the case.

A Possible Explanation

In his book The Beast on Wall Street, Dr. Robert Haugen argues that investors muck up market behavior with their emotions, so the news of the day frequently results in market moves that don’t make sense. Markets go up on bad news and down on good news.

During the Summer, many traders are on vacation so less mucking up in July through August. But then the mucking starts again when they return to their desks in September. Make sense?

Place Your Bets

As shown in the following table from Lazy Portfolio ETF, sorted by best-to-worst September returns, winning and losing asset classes vary by month. In the case of September:

  • Gold and International Bonds are the best performing asset classes in September with a .45% average return. Gold has delivered positive returns in September 83% of the time – very good odds. The best month for gold is January, and the worst is March. International Bonds have delivered positive September returns 68% of the time. December is the best month for this asset class and April is the worst month.
  • US Technology stocks have the worst September performance, losing -0.79% on average. These stocks win 52% of the time in September, so not an even odds bet. February is the worst month for this asset class. January is the best month.

You can use this table every month to see where the odds are in and out of your favor.

Changing Fortunes

Coming into 2025, US stocks had dominated for the previous 15 years as the best performing asset class, so diversification beyond US stocks did not “work” -- it undermined performance. But that has changed so far this year. As shown in the following, US stocks are not the best performing so far this year, so diversification into foreign securities and gold has added value.

Investments in other assets have benefited performance, as can be seen in the performance of target date funds (TDFs). In the past 15 years, until now, diversification did not work because US stocks dominated.

Conclusion

This August is likely (64% probability) to deliver a positive US stock market return, plus US stocks have been the best performing asset class in August, so justification for staying the course.

But the historical odds place this September’s US stock market at a disadvantage with a 50/50 chance of a loss, but you can turn the odds more in your favor by overweighting gold and foreign bonds and underweighting US Technology stocks based on their September history of successes and failures.

Good luck!!

data.title: AMD Q2: Rating Downgrade Amid China And Opex Uncertainties
data.description:
AMD stock: previous thesis and new developments

I last rated Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) as a buy on June 27 with an article titled " AMD: Let Your Profit Run.” The article was promoted by the strong Q1 results the company reported, which “indicated robust growth in data center and client segments.”

Since then, the company has released its earnings report (ER) for FQ2 of 2025. The ER provided several materials updates on its financial and operational performance, motivating this follow-up analysis. In the end, my overall conclusion is that the developments in Q2 have weakened my earlier bullish thesis, thus leading to a rating downgrade to hold under current conditions. In the remainder of this article, I will elaborate on the top 2 concerns I see from the ER: the uncertainties AMD faces in terms of its opex and also the export license in the Chinese license market.

Let me start with the top and bottom lines reported for Q2. As seen, its normalized EPS is in line with the Street’s estimate. Sales came in with a $300 million beat. However, digging into the ER closely, a few factors led me to question fundamental drivers of the beat. First, a large portion of the sales beat in Q2 came from its game consoles per my estimation, not the data center. Second, I also suspect that the intra-quarter stock run-up played a sizable role in the beat since July 15, when China's potential licensing news came out.

This factor also leads me to a bigger concern regarding the licensing issue, as detailed in the next section.

AMD stock: China uncertainties and competition dynamics with NVDA

Judging by the updates provided in the ER, I see no guarantee when AMD will receive its license to resume its shipments to the Chinese market. Even if/when that happens, I expect the company will need some time (probably 3 quarters or even a year) to get its supply chain fully back on track. These uncertainties and associated costs are very likely to further pressure AMD’s profits given the competition dynamics with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

In a nutshell, my view is that NVDA is much better positioned than AMD to navigate the uncertainties of ongoing export control uncertainties between the U.S. and China. As a reflection, the two charts below display the significant disparities in their profitability and operational efficiency, where NVDA consistently outperforms AMD by a wide margin. For instance, NVDA’s gross profit margin stands at 70.11%, a substantial lead over AMD’s 50.99%. This trend continues down the income statement, with NVDA’s EBIT margin at 58.03% versus AMD’s 8.29%, its EBITDA margin at 59.42% compared to AMD’s 18.46%, and its net income margin at 51.69% versus AMD’s 9.57%.

Turning to the second chart, in terms of quarterly fixed asset turnover rates, NVDA also features far superior efficiency over AMD. While AMD's fixed asset turnover has improved significantly to the current reading of 3.796 in the past few quarters, it is still a far cry from NVDA’s current level of 5.177. Also note that the pace of NVDA’s improvement over the past few years or so – surging from ~1.2 in early 2023 to the current 5.177 - has also been far quicker than AMD.

As AMD and the sector navigate the export control uncertainties (or trade uncertainties in general), I expect NVDA’s significantly higher profit margins and better operating metrics to serve as a critical buffer against potential revenue disruptions or increased costs for supply chain reshuffling. In a scenario where U.S.-China trade tensions lead to a loss of a key market or necessitate a costly reconfiguration of supply chains, I expect NVDA to enjoy far more flexibility to absorb the financial impact without severely damaging its profitability. In contrast, AMD’s much tighter margins leave it far more vulnerable. Additionally, its inferior fixed asset turnover suggests that it is less efficiently leveraging its physical capital, a key indicator of operational resilience.

Given the large disparity in this metric, I expect NVDA to more quickly adapt its manufacturing and distribution processes to circumvent new trade barriers, whereas AMD's lower turnover will slow its ability to mitigate such changes.

AMD stock: Opex concerns

A second key concern on my mind after reading the ER is the continued rise of opex. As an example, the chart below shows AMD's latest income statement. As seen, just in the past four quarters alone, its operating expenses have increased substantially. To wit, total operating expenses have risen from $2,928 million in the September 2024 quarter to $3,453 million in Q2 2025. This increase is driven primarily by R&D and SG&A expenses.

Looking ahead, given the competition pressure from NVDA, I see large odds for AMD to keep maintaining high opex intensity. For example, NVDA is actively ramping up its Blackwell Ultra systems as we speak, and I further expect it to launch the next-gen Rubin platform sometime in FY 2026. While AMD has reported significant progress on its new platforms in Q2, my assessment is that AMD still lacks experience in the system/rack approach. For AMD to build a system/rack on a meaningful scale to compete with NVDA on this front, I won’t be surprised to see continued opex pressure (and most likely accompanied by some capex pressure too).

Other risks and final thoughts

Despite the disparity in profitability and operating metrics, AMD is currently priced at about the same P/E as NVDA thanks to the strong rallies of stock prices in the past 2~3 months (another factor that helped to trigger this follow-up analysis). As seen, in terms of FY1 Non-GAAP P/E ratios, AMD’s reading stands at 41.78 currently, vs. 41.70 for NVDA.

To keep the discussion balanced, let me close by highlighting some of the positives I see from the ER. After all, I am recommending investors to hold AMD, not sell it. At the top of my list is the progress with its AI GPU pipeline. I continue to see strong execution on this front with the MI355X (which can directly compete with NVDA’s Blackwell in my view). Per the ER, these chips are on track to ramp up volume in the second half of FY 2025. Additionally, I am optimistic that the MI400 Series (Rubin-compete) and its Helios rack system are also on track to hit the market soon (most likely sometime in FY 2026). These new product lineups could pull in significant sales to help offset potential revenue losses either from China or elsewhere.

To conclude, this AMD follow-up is motivated by the business updates described in its Q2 ER, in particular the opex pressure and also the licensing uncertainties in the China market. After considering these new developments, especially against the competing dynamics against NVDA, I see them as far stronger headwinds for AMD in the near term compared to Nvidia. These considerations have weakened my earlier bullish arguments materially and led to a rating downgrade to hold under the current conditions.

data.title: Air Products And Chemicals: Solid Execution And Upside Ahead
data.description:
We return to provide our commentary on Air Products and Chemicals (NYSE:APD) in light of the company’s Q3 results. Here at the Lab, we expect a positive reaction to the financial performance, though we see some fodder for both sides of the debate. Even if we remain supportive, we highlighted two negative debates in our previous analysis: 1) results relative to peers and 2) the company's CEO succession plan. Q3 results came in solid, and the company has now completed its CEO transition.

Today, we (once again) reaffirmed our long-standing Buy view, and we continue to regard Air Products as a mispriced first mover in the energy transition and hydrogen economy, with its substantial project backlog and dividend aristocrat profile. The ongoing investment cycle in next‑generation technologies creates a temporary valuation discount versus peers. At the same time, the core industrial gas business continues to generate strong cash flows, making APD a compelling blend of value and future‑oriented growth in specialty chemicals. This is the core of our investment thesis.

Q3 Results and Our Positive Take

The company reported top-line sales of $3.02 billion, up 1% year-over-year. This result was mainly driven by higher pricing (energy cost pass-through) and partially offset by lower volumes. The price was up by +1% at the group level, including headwinds in the helium price. In Q3, we report a lower COGS, and the company delivered an operating income of $790.6 million, compared to $737.6 million last year. Core segments in the EU and Asia performed well, with operating incomes of 10% and 8%, respectively, supported by substantial on-site volumes. Looking at the GEO specifics, Americas volumes were down 6% on a yearly basis. Volumes were down due to lower helium and onsite demand. The segment's core operating profit reached $374 million. Lower volumes were offset by margin improvement, with a margin of 29.7%. Asia volume was up 2%, and the company started up new assets, but the results were dampened by lower helium. Despite lower volumes/price, the segment's core operating profit reached $217 million. In Europe, APD's volumes were up 3% thanks to higher onsite demand. The segment EBIT reached $225 million with a margin of 29.2%. Adjusted EPS declined by 3% to $3.09, impacted by the absence of LNG earnings and one-time tax benefits from the prior year.

Source: APD Q3 results ppt - Fig 1

Why Are We Still Positive?

  1. In line with our constructive view on APD, we note a recent FT feature highlighting the remarkable visibility of this otherwise ‘invisible industry with a hi-vis future.’ In a nutshell, the article reported that the sector offers exceptional earnings visibility, underpinned by 15–20‑year contracts and is largely insulated from political fluctuations. According to Visible Alpha’s aggregated estimates, the big three (Linde, APD, and Air Liquide - all rated by our team) are expected to deliver EBITDA growth of 3–6% this year, accelerating to 6–7% next year;
  2. Operating margins expanded by roughly 300 basis points quarter‑on‑quarter (Fig. 1). While such a jump is unlikely to recur each period, it signals early progress in APD’s cost‑efficiency initiatives. In the Americas, volumes declined about 6% year‑over‑year, partly reflecting the impact of project exits. This is a piece of supportive evidence. Some volume weakness indicates that exiting volumes add minimal or no earnings, supporting ROIC and EBIT margin improvement. At this stage, there were no other significant changes in cost optimization, and capex guidance remains unchanged. As a reminder, this is the first quarter for the new CEO. So, we might wait-and-see what's next and look for catalysts over the next six-nine months to de-risk APD's larger projects;
  3. APD Q4 EPS is at $3.35, with Wall Street expectations in the mid-range of the outlook range. The company's yearly EPS has narrowed to $12 with a 24% EBIT margin (Fig. 2). In Q3, at the aggregate level, the company delivered a core operating profit margin of 24.5%. So, we might expect some upside ahead;
  4. The company is progressing with its NEOM project, which will be the World’s largest green supplier of hydrogen. APD reported 80% construction completion. We regard the NEOM project as a pioneering development in the green hydrogen industry, with APD already signaling the potential for share repurchases alongside its ongoing dividend growth policy (Fig. 3).

Fig 2

Fig 3

Earnings Change and Valuation

Applying a $12 EPS estimate and a 25x P/E multiple, in line with APD’s historical average, we arrive at a target price of $300 per share, implying only a modest upside from the current share price level. Looking forward, the company has historically delivered annual EPS growth of roughly 6–7%, supported by its CAPEX program, which remains unchanged (at $5 billion) and continues to underpin long‑term expansion. With the new CEO and already Q3 evidence on lower growth with upside on profit generation, APD is progressing in "optimizing underperforming projects," so we anticipate a 2026 EPS growth of at least 5% to $12.6. Another key consideration is the company’s free cash flow. Over the past three years, Air Products has averaged negative free cash flows, primarily due to significant CAPEX. In our view, the key to valuing this company lies in properly modeling these CAPEX-heavy cash flows. Using a simple adjustment, if we isolate only the recurring free cash flows, the company would show a free cash flow yield of approximately >3%, which aligns with the industry average. Therefore, we continue to use a 25x P/E target, and with our 2026 EPS estimates set, we set our target price at $315 per share. Linde and Air Liquide trade with a P/E of 28x and >30x, respectively. So, there is still a valuation gap.

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) higher-than-anticipated CAPEX costs, 2) higher restructuring costs with a negative one-off charge under the new CEO, 3) higher corporate costs with wage inflation, 4) lower-than-anticipated JV income; 5) COGS volatility; and 6) earnings disappointments. Additionally, APD relies on long-term significant CAPEX investments, so there is an execution risk to consider. If any of these projects fail or do not deliver within the timeframe, this would lead to a considerable pushback against the company. While the company was resilient against its headwinds this quarter due to decent energy pass-through, in reality, the volumes dropped by 4%. APD operates ~465 facilities in the Americas, with only ~25% on owned land, while in Asia and Europe, ~35% of sites are owned or held through long-term grants. US limited property ownership is another potential risk.

Conclusion

We saw Air Products and Chemicals deliver a step-up in EBIT margin in Q3, and even though it reported a volume decline, this did not affect earnings. It seems that the new CEO is already moving in the right direction. We view ADP as the leading chemical player, and this valuation gap will decrease over time. With the new buyback potential announcement, this will provide the buy signal for Wall Street. In the meantime, we remain buyers.

data.title: Dividend Growth Investing Vs. The Market - Who Wins? (Part 2)
data.description:
In Part 1 of this two-part series, we explored how dividend growth ETFs compared to the broader market over the long term and during the so-called Lost Decade (2000–2010). Spoiler: dividend growth investors fared much better when things got rough.

Now, let’s explore how dividend growth strategies held up during two of the most defining periods of recent market history: the 2008 financial crisis and the post-COVID rollercoaster. Then we’ll wrap things up with some powerful takeaways to help you choose a strategy that fits you.

The 2008 Financial Crisis

The global meltdown of 2008 exposed flaws in the banking system and reminded investors how painful bear markets can be.

Let’s look at how our benchmark ETFs performed from January 2008 to 2015.

U.S. Market vs. Dividend Growth (2008–2015)

  • S&P 500 (SPY): $100,000 grew to $163,000
  • Dividend Growth (VDIGX): $100,000 grew to $179,000

Canadian Market vs. Dividend Growth (2010–2015)*

  • TSX (XIU:CA): $100,000 grew to $137,000
  • Dividend Growth (CDZ:CA): $100,000 grew to $183,000

*Note: CDZ’s data begins in 2010, so the comparison starts post-crisis.

Despite the turmoil (U.S. government shutdowns, European debt crises, and endless market panic), dividend growth investors ended up with stronger results and lower drawdowns. That consistency is a major advantage for retirees or anyone drawing income from their portfolio.

And just like we saw during the Lost Decade, dividend payers didn’t just recover—they led the way back.

Since January 2020: COVID and the Recovery

Here’s where things get even more interesting.

From a historical standpoint, 2020 to 2025 might be one of the wildest five-year periods in market history. We went from global lockdowns and market freefall to one of the fastest recoveries ever—followed by high inflation, aggressive rate hikes, and a tech resurgence.

So how did the ETFs perform?

U.S. Market vs. Dividend Growth (2020–2025)

  • S&P 500 (SPY): $100,000 grew to $198,000
  • Dividend Growth (VDIGX): $100,000 grew to $160,000

Canadian Market vs. Dividend Growth (2020–2025)

  • TSX (XIU:CA): $100,000 grew to $182,000
  • Dividend Growth (CDZ:CA): $100,000 grew to $160,000

This time, the index outperformed. Especially in the U.S., where mega-cap tech names like Apple, Microsoft, and NVIDIA (a.k.a. the Magnificent Seven) pulled the market to new highs.

Interestingly, the Canadian dividend ETF underperformed even the TSX. This is unusual, and may be explained by its exposure to sectors like REITs—which suffered during the COVID crash and were slow to recover amid higher interest rates.

Still, the dividend-focused portfolios delivered solid double-digit gains—and with less volatility. If your goal is to sleep well at night while still growing your wealth, that’s a win.

So, Who Wins?

Let’s break down the lessons from this two-part series:

Dividend Growth Wins When…

  • The market is falling (e.g., 2000–2010, 2008 crisis)
  • You prioritize consistency, lower volatility, and income
  • You value owning businesses you understand and believe in

The Market Index Wins When…

  • We’re in a raging bull market (e.g., post-2009 tech run, 2023+ recovery)
  • You’re focused solely on maximizing total return
  • You’re comfortable with higher drawdowns and more volatility

In other words, dividend growth doesn’t always outperform. But it does shine in the moments that matter most—when fear is highest and investors are second-guessing everything.

If you want your portfolio to be something you can stick with through thick and thin, dividend growth has a powerful edge.

My Real-Life Results

If you’re wondering how to put dividend growth theory into practice, here’s a real example. Better yet, I’ve outlined my approach step-by-step in the Investment Roadmap—the very process I’ve followed to manage my portfolio since 2017.

In my own pension portfolio (started in 2017), I’ve followed a strict dividend growth strategy. It’s outperformed a 50/50 blend of XIU.TO and VFV.TO (used to reflect currency-adjusted index performance). And I did that with less volatility—even with a few losing picks along the way (looking at you, Algonquin and Sylogist).

What carried me through wasn’t picking all the winners—it was staying true to the process. And that’s what dividend growth investing is all about.

You can follow it through my monthly updates:

The Final Word: Stay Invested

Here’s the most important insight of all:

No one who stayed invested was a loser.

Whether you went with dividend stocks, the S&P 500, or a mix of both—if you stuck with it, you won.

If you’re still debating which strategy is right for you, ask yourself this: Which one will I stay loyal to when the market drops 20%?

Because the market will drop again. And if you’ve built your plan around predictable income, high-quality businesses, and a clear strategy… you’ll sleep a lot better when it does.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wasatch Core Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

U.S. small-cap stocks ended the quarter with sizable gains, but the march forward was volatile. Initially, stocks turned lower after President Trump unveiled tariff rates for countries that were much higher than investors had anticipated. However, equities then rebounded sharply as the President's economic team appeared to be softening its stance on tariffs. For the quarter, the Russell 2000® Growth Index was up 11.97%,while the Wasatch Core Growth Fund-Investor Class gained 5.57%.

As investor views on tariffs shifted, a "risk-on" environment followed for much of the quarter. Against this backdrop, the stocks of lower-quality companies generally outperformed those of higher-quality businesses. This was a headwind for our investment style, which focuses on high-quality companies. And that headwind was severe. By some metrics, this was one of the strongest environments favoring low-quality stocks that we've experienced.

For perspective, one way we gauge whether high-quality or low-quality stocks are in favor is through a proprietary process, in which we screen and rank the small-cap universe by several aggregate factors including financial metrics such as return on assets and operating profit margins, among other items. We then compare how the companies in the top quintile of this aggregate score (high-quality businesses) perform against the bottom quintile (low-quality businesses). Based on that metric, this was the third-strongest 10-week period of outperformance by low-quality stocks of the last decade, and the strongest since early 2021.

Details of the quarter

The largest detractor from Fund performance was BellRing Brands, Inc. (BRBR). The company provides various nutritional products and is best known for its Premier Protein and Dymatize brands. BellRing's fundamentals are strong, but there is a one-off channel inventory dynamic affecting guidance for the third quarter, which caused the stock to sell off. We visited the company recently, and believe our secular thesis is on track given strong consumption metrics and a continued runway to increase distribution and penetrate new households.

Saia, Inc. (SAIA) also detracted. Saia is a trucking company focused on the high margin "less than a full truckload" (LTL) space. Investments the company made in logistics and operations have enabled it to take market share in recent years, and the stock price had benefited. But the stock dropped significantly in late April after Saia announced surprisingly weak quarterly results, citing weather-related disruptions and weaker-than-expected March shipments. Investors look to the results of LTL companies as an early signal of the health of the broader trucking industry. We spoke with management following the earnings announcement and remain confident in the company's ability to continue to outgrow weaker competitors.

Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc. (CWAN) was another detractor. Clearwater Analytics offers a software platform for investment accounting, compliance, risk management and reporting that gives asset managers and insurers daily insights into portfolio holdings. During the quarter, the stock reacted negatively as the company completed its acquisition of a software provider for the investment management and hedge fund industry. We continue to like Clearwater but lowered the stock's weight in the Fund as we assess what the acquisition might mean for Clearwater's business.

Two of the top contributors to Fund performance were technology companies, Fabrinet (FN) and Nova Ltd. (NVMI). Fabrinet makes optical components that are used in data centers. Nova sells metrology and process control systems used in semiconductor manufacturing. Both businesses have benefited from the increasing use of artificial intelligence (AI). While fundamentals for the two companies remain strong, AI has been a popular investment theme and that likely played a larger role in fueling the stocks' rise.

Another top contributor for the Fund was Wingstop, Inc. (WING). The firm operates and franchises a network of more than 2,500 fast-casual restaurants, specializing in cooked-to-order, hand-sauced and tossed chicken wings. Wingstop released what we considered to be a solid set of operating results when it reported earnings in late April. The stock rose on the back of those results. We like Wingstop for its highly profitable franchising model, which generates significant revenue from royalties and franchise fees. We also like the potential of Wingstop's new Al-driven kitchens, which use data to forecast the demand for wings. By forecasting demand early, wings can be prepared sooner, increasing throughput at the store and driving higher average unit volumes (AUVs). Wingstop is trying these new kitchens in test markets and the results have been encouraging. (Current and future holdings are subject to change and risk.)

Outlook and positioning

As we look toward the back half of the year, we wouldn't be surprised to see markets stay volatile. As we write this commentary at the end of June, there is still a great deal of uncertainty about tariffs and trade policy, and wars in Europe and the Middle East. Throw in the uncertainty about interest rate policy and there are plenty of factors that could shake the equity markets.

What those issues don't shake is our confidence in our investment philosophy, process and positioning. We remain focused on high-quality companies that have a proven track record of earnings growth, a history of strong free cash-flow generation, and that are led by management teams we know and trust.

That high-quality focus was admittedly a headwind to the Fund's performance relative to its benchmark this quarter. But in times of macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, these are precisely the types of businesses we want to own.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Paul Lambert, Mike Valentine and Kipling Weisel

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the data quoted. For the most recent month-end performance data, visit wasatchglobal.com. Average Unit Volume (AUV) is the average annual sales generated by each location within a franchise.

Earnings growth is a measure of growth in a company's net income over a specific period, often one year.

Operating margin, also called operating profit margin, is the amount a company makes on every dollar of sales after deducting variable costs but before accounting for interest or taxes. Operating margin is calculated by dividing the company's operating income by its net sales.

Return on assets (ROA) measures a company's profitability by showing how many dollars of earnings a company derives from each dollar of assets it controls.

data.title: Omega Flex: A Robust Business, But Fairly Valued
data.description:
Introduction

Omega Flex (OFLX) is a manufacturer of flexible metal hose, which is mainly used to carry gases and liquids in, for instance, automotive and marina fuel applications, in residential and industrial applications. The company has manufacturing capacity in the United States and in the United Kingdom, which allows it to serve both the American and European markets.

The First Half Of The Year Was Pretty Decent

In the second quarter of the current financial year, Omega Flex reported a total revenue of approximately $25.5M. After deducting the $10.1M in COGS, its reported gross profit was approximately $15.4M. This represents an increase of approximately 1% compared to the second quarter of the previous financial year.

As the income statement below shows, the company unfortunately had to deal with increased operating expenses, which weighed on the operating profit, resulting in a 10% decrease. Additionally, the net interest income decreased as well, but the company was able to keep the damage limited and reported a pre-tax income of roughly $5.4M.

This resulted in a net profit of approximately $4.1M, and after deducting the net income attributable to the non-controlling interests, the net income for the common shareholders was approximately $4.16M for an EPS of $0.41.

This brought the net income in the first half of the financial year to $7.7M, for a net profit per share of approximately $0.77. This means the dividends of $0.34 per quarter were still fully covered based on the company's reported net profit in both quarters.

And although the company continues to incur a total amount of capital expenditures that’s higher than the depreciation expenses, its cash flow result remains very strong.

As you can see below, Omega Flex reported a total operating cash flow of approximately $7M, which included an investment and working capital position to the tune of approximately $1.35M. This means the underlying operating cash flow was roughly $8.5M.

We see the total amount of capital expenditures incurred in the first semester was almost exactly $1M, which means the underlying free cash flow was approximately $7.5M for an FCFPS of $0.74. Excluding the impact of the lower net tax liabilities and the reduction of tax liabilities beyond what was owed in H1 2025, the underlying FCFPS was approximately $0.75.

The Balance Sheet Remains A Fortress

While it is quite understandable investors are not very interested in paying a multiple of approximately 20 times the net income for Omega Flex, it's worth emphasizing the company has a fortress-like balance sheet.

At the end of the second quarter, Omega Flex had in excess of $50 million in cash and zero debt. As you can see below, the majority of the liabilities on the balance sheet are related to trade payables and liabilities, lease liabilities, and an accrued but unpaid dividend payment to the common shareholders.

The company ended the quarter with a total working capital position of approximately $67M, which represents approximately 20% of the current market capitalization. In fact, based on the current share count and cash position, Omega Flex is currently trading at an enterprise value of approximately $276M. And considering the company reported an underlying EBITDA of $9.8M in H1 2025 and a FY 2024 EBITDA of approximately $24.6M, the valuation is relatively reasonable for a company in its position.

Investment Thesis

Unfortunately, a ‘reasonable’ valuation does not warrant a ‘buy’ recommendation. The company has a good position in an interesting niche market, but considering the stock is trading at approximately 20 times earnings and at a double-digit EV/EBITDA multiple, I don't really see any alpha here.

I will keep an eye on the company and the stock, but I’m just keeping it as a ‘hold’ right now.

data.title: Global Payments: Solid Cash Flow, Deep Discount, Manageable Risks
data.description:
Thesis

My research argues that Global Payments Inc.'s (NYSE: NYSE:GPN) accelerating pivot toward high-margin, software-centric commerce enablement has outpaced the market’s perception of its value. They’ve been selling off their older, lower-profit divisions, and their most recent earnings show that these changes are already boosting profit margins and cash flow. Yet the stock market still prices the company like nothing has improved, its shares are much cheaper than both its own long-term average and other companies in the same industry. Unless management seriously messes up the plan, this mismatch between how well the business is doing and how low the stock price is could be a great buying opportunity for patient investors.

How Global Payments Is Reshaping Its Business Model

Global Payments is going through a major transformation in the mid-2020s, and it's not by accident. The company is deliberately reshaping itself, step by step, to move away from being a broad, complex payments business and become something more focused and streamlined: a specialist in helping businesses accept payments and run their operations more efficiently, what they call “commerce enablement."

I think one of the clearest signals of this shift came in late May when Global Payments said that it would sell its Payroll division to Acrisure for $1.1 billion that's expected to close in the second half of 2025, assuming it gets the green light from regulators. Overall, the sale is part of a pattern. The company has also sold off other business lines like AdvancedMD, a healthcare tech platform, and is divesting its Issuer Solutions segment, which manages credit and debit card programs for banks.

The company has said it plans to return the money from the Payroll sale to shareholders, likely through stock buybacks, while still keeping its debt levels in check, and that matters a lot. That's because, just a few weeks before the Payroll deal, GPN announced it would buy Worldpay for a $24.25 billion. That’s a massive deal by any measure and one that significantly increases the company’s debt.

Investors didn’t love the timing, or the price tag. On the day the Worldpay deal was announced, GPN shares dropped over 17%, which tells you just how concerned they were. Basically, the sharp selloff conveyed worries about whether GPN could successfully merge a business that large and whether it was taking on too much debt too fast.

Altogether, these three deals, the Payroll division, AdvancedMD, and its plans to exit its Issuer Solutions business will remove over $550 million in yearly revenue from the company’s books. That might sound like a big loss, but management's take was that these businesses weren’t great fits anymore. They were lower-margin operations and they didn’t align with where the company wants to go strategically.

My take is, by offloading these parts, the company is trying to simplify itself and focus more tightly on its core mission, the result is a cleaner, merchant-centric profile emphasizing acquiring, omnichannel facilitation, and integrated software. In theory, the logic is straightforward. Fewer moving parts and clearer revenue drivers should boost operating leverage and investor comprehension, but the capital markets have yet to give the plan their blessing.

Per it's 1Y price price performance, GPN' stock fell 15.38%, even as the broader S&P 500 (SP500) rose 21.09% over the same period. The skepticism shows up in the rest of the momentum scores too meaning that this isn’t just a case of the stock slipping for a few weeks, it’s been under pressure for a while, and the market has been unconvinced that the company’s changes will deliver near-term rewards.

Seeking Alpha's GPN peer comparison also sharpens that contrast. Over roughly the same twelve-month span, Affirm (AFRM) was the top performer here, and even Jack Henry (JKHY), which I typically regard as a conservative mainstay, managed to eke out a marginal positive return. Against this backdrop, GPN looks like an outlier, left behind while the rest of the sector rallies. But something changed in mid-July (and arguably after yesterday's 9% post-earnings gain too) that could signal a turning point. The high-profile activist investor Elliott Management disclosed that it had taken a significant stake in Global, and their arrival suggests that someone with both influence and a track record sees opportunity here, and that could be the catalyst the company needs to get back on investors’ radar.

Global Payments Q2: Cash Flow, Margins, and Momentum

I'd categorize Global Payments Q2 as steady, especially given that the company is in the middle of a major business overhaul. The company earned $3.10 per share, which was 4 cents more than what Wall Street expected, and it brought in $2.36 billion in revenue, which slightly beat forecasts by about $281,000. That revenue was also up 1.6% compared to the same time last year. Meanwhile, its adjusted operating margin, increased by 1.3 percentage points, landing at 44.6%. That’s a strong margin in this sector. Breaking that down further, the Merchant Solutions segment hit an even higher margin at 50.1%, and Issuer Solutions jumped to 48.7%, after a 1.9 percentage point improvement.

GPN continues to generate strong free cash flow, too. The company realized about $800 million in adjusted free cash flow, which is 110% of its adjusted net income. For the year so far, the company has converted 95% of its net income into free cash flow, which is a sign of healthy financial operations. At the same time, in terms of managing that debt, its leverage ratio was 3.15x. The company expects that to drop to 3.0x by the end of the year, giving it more breathing room to meet its goal of returning $7.5 billion to shareholders by 2027, a return, by the way, that will likely come through buybacks and possibly dividends.

On top of that, management raised its estimate for annual cost savings from its ongoing business transformation. They now expect those improvements to generate $650 million in yearly operating income, which is $100 million higher than what they had previously projected. And finally, one minor headwind is improving: currency exchange rates, which generally have an impact on earnings for companies that operate internationally, are now expected to be a 0.50% drag, instead of the 1.25% hit the company had projected earlier.

Inside the revenue engine, the revitalized U.S. salesforce began to reflect its new incentive framework. 90% of core payment sellers have shifted to the updated plan, sales per representative have advanced by high single digits, new-hire productivity is 10% higher, and attrition is lower. Point-of-sale sales shot up 30% sequentially, suggesting that training expenditures and adjustments to remuneration are producing tangible volume. However, the fact that just 20% of the team is now qualified to market Genius, the new flagship platform, suggests that there is still a lot of room for growth as certification expands.

That said, the most well-known product story of the year comes from Genius itself. Genius Restaurant, which opened in May, brought in more restaurant customers quickly, bringing the total to the mid-teens. Genius Retail, which opened a month later, helped U.S. direct-channel sales rise 37% over the previous year while doubling wholesale-channel wins. Internationally the platform is live in 13 European Union markets, including a recent iOS deployment in Spain, and is making early headway with SoftPOS and tap-to-pay functionality. Merchant Solutions, the primary growth pillar, produced $1.83B of adjusted revenue, a 5.5% increase excluding dispositions. Point-of-sale and integrated embedded subsegments each posted high-single-digit gains, while 85 new ISV partners joined during the quarter. There's clear evidence of geographic breadth here as Central Europe, Latin America, and Asia-Pacific all saw sales increase in the high single digits.

Despite being marked for future sale, Issuer Solutions increased adjusted revenue by 3.5% to $547M and added 15M accounts, bringing the total number of accounts on file to over 900M. Four letters of intent and six further implementations are pending, ensuring short-term continuity as this gets played out. In July, the imminent Worldpay purchase, which is expected to close in the first half of 2026, passed the U.S. antitrust examination. Planning for integration has already started, and management is still supporting synergy estimates that would result in a processor that can handle 100 billion transactions annually and $4 trillion in volume, a scale that is unmatched in the payments industry.

Finally, in regard to capital-allocation policy, beyond $690M of 2025 buybacks already completed, the board approved a $500M accelerated share-repurchase program funded by the Payroll proceeds. Perspective-wise, with $3B of liquidity and 95% of debt fixed at an average 3.5% coupon, the company is definitely threading the needle here between fortifying competitive assets and rewarding shareholders.

Global Payments: A Deep Discount on Earnings and Cash Flow

Altogether, when you look at Global's day-to-day business performance, I think it's fair to say that things seem pretty solid. But the way the stock is priced in the market looks much more pessimistic. As of my research, the stock's price reflects a blended P/E ratio of just 7.19. For comparison, Global Payments’ historical average P/E is closer to 15, and the typical P/E across the sector is around 11.

Looking ahead, it gets even more extreme. Based on analyst estimates, the P/E is expected to fall to 7.04 this year, 6.27 in 2026, and just 5.50 in 2027. But those assumptions don’t line up with what the company is actually doing, it’s growing earnings, generating strong cash flow, and making strategic changes to focus on its strongest areas.

According to FAST Graphs, the stock’s fair value should be around $178.52 right now. And if GPN keeps growing earnings at the current projected pace, the model suggests the stock could reach about $232.74 by 2028. That sets up a potential annual return of nearly 12% for investors, driven not only by steady earnings growth but also by the possibility that the market eventually realizes the company is worth a higher P/E multiple than it’s getting today. Even just based on current earnings, the EPS yield is almost 14%, which is unusually high and suggests the stock is extremely undervalued.

Cash-flow analysis amplifies the disconnect. Free cash flow per share is forecast to climb 23.45%, roughly double the sector average, while levered free cash flow rose 85.66% year on year, more than 8x the peer median. Operating cash flow advanced 63.31% year on year and is projected to add another 15+% forward, reflecting a capital-light model increasingly focused on margin capture.

Although headline revenue growth is muted, 3.23% trailing, 4.21% forward, I'd argue that that figure obscures the intentional revenue shrinkage from the divested units and what remains is higher-quality turnover tied to Merchant Solutions and cloud-native software, where margins and lifetime-value characteristics should exceed the shed businesses.

And the earnings illustrate that shift: adjusted EPS expanded 22.93% over the past year, with 9–14% annual increases projected through 2027. On a GAAP basis EPS jumped 63.4%, with a 25% forward outlook.

Return on equity climbed 20.09% year over year, and the forward non-GAAP PEG ratio of 0.47 sits 62% below the sector median, implying that the stock is very cheap relative to how fast its earnings are expected to grow, even if growth slows a bit, the price still looks like a bargain when you consider how much cash the business is generating.

What’s Holding Global Payments Back

Still, challenges remain. For starters, revenue only grew 1.6% over the past year, and 5% if you adjust for currency and recent business sales. That may sound okay on its own, but it’s weak compared to other companies in the digital payments space, many of which are seeing double-digit growth.

During the earnings call, management admitted that the broader economic environment is uncertain, and that consumer confidence is still low. For a company like Global Payments that earns money based on how much money moves through its systems, that’s an important headwind.

There are also some growing pains from the company’s transformation strategy. One example is Genius. Right now, a lot of Genius’s momentum so far is coming from new customer sign-ups, not from existing customers upgrading to it. I think that's important because getting current clients (brand loyalty) to add more services is usually more profitable than landing brand-new ones. But so far, that “cross-selling” hasn’t kicked in at scale, so the company is still waiting to tap into that extra value. Before Genius officially launched, some customers were holding off on buying decisions, waiting to see what the final product would look like. That kind of pause can delay sales, which then pushes some revenue into future quarters instead of the one we’re in now.

And lastly, as Genius expands internationally, it gets more complicated. Each country has its own rules for things like taxes, receipts, and compliance, what’s known as “fiscalization.” That means Global Payments has to invest extra time and money just to make sure the platform works correctly in each new market and these kinds operational hurdles can slow down the rollout and increase costs.

There’s also another point to be made since it was brought a few times and that comes to how GPN is returning money to shareholders. Earlier this year, the company said it expected to have about 289 million shares outstanding. But by the end of the quarter, that number had dropped to around 280 million, and buybacks can be good in the short term because they reduce the number of shares in circulation, which helps boost EPS. But they also make it harder to tell whether earnings growth is truly coming from better business performance or just from having fewer shares to divide the profits among. In other words, buybacks can mask weak growth if the company is using them as a substitute for actual revenue and profit improvement.

Maybe this is stretch, but I think there’s also some risk if the company focuses too much on buying back shares instead of investing in new products, technologies, or services. That is, if the economy weakens or if the integration of Worldpay takes longer than expected, the decision to put money into buybacks rather than organic reinvestment could come back to haunt them if growth stalls and investors start questioning long-term priorities.

Final Takeaway

Buy. In my view, the stock’s ~7× P/E and double-digit free-cash-flow growth leave it far too cheap given expanding margins, rising cost-savings targets, and potential catalysts. I’m willing to own it here for re-rating upside, but I reserve “Strong Buy” until the Worldpay integration and elevated leverage prove they can be digested without derailing execution.
